preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiced labio-velar approximant ([w]) to a velar nasal ([ŋ]) is an attested, though often phonetically conditioned, sound change. This shift involves a change in the manner of articulation from an approximant to a nasal, and a change in the place of articulation from labio-velar to velar. It typically occurs in environments where nasalization is present or spreading.
phoneticEffects: Nasalization, place assimilation (labio-velar to velar), change in manner of articulation (approximant to nasal)

examples.note: Observed as a sound correspondence in IY, a language related to Kiliji and Yoruba, indicating a shift in progress from [w] to [ŋ].
The labial-velar approximant [w] is fully nasalized into [ŋ] when immediately preceded by a labial nasal consonant ([m] or [n]) and immediately followed by a nasal vowel.

sources: Brindle, J. (2015). Kiliji, An Unrecorded Spiritual Language of Eastern Ghana. Journal of West African Languages, 42(1), 65-80.
Lionnet, F. A. J. (2016). Subphonemic Teamwork: A Typology and Theory of Cumulative Coarticulatory Effects in Phonology. University of California, Berkeley.
